skill.name: task-planning
skill.description: Plan agile software work with user stories, acceptance criteria, priorities, sprint scope, and task board structure.
skill.summary: Break features into clear user stories, backlog items, and sprint plans.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance findings were false positives from Markdown fences and ordinary planning examples. The skill contains no executable scripts and no prompt injection language. Two external reference links are present, so network exposure is limited to user-visible documentation links.

low_findings.title: Benign External Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The skill includes two hardcoded URLs in a References section. They are visible educational links and do not transmit data or trigger automatic network activity.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are plain Markdown reference links. No evidence found of hidden requests, credential handling, or automatic network execution.

low_findings.title: False Positive Command Execution Detections
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown code fences and inline tags used to document output formats and examples. They are not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks appear inside documentation examples and tag formatting. No executable Ruby, shell script, or command interpolation was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Matches
low_findings.description: The high-risk weak crypto and reconnaissance matches are ordinary task-planning terms, including description text, UI design tasks, and requirement examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is semantic planning content, not cryptographic implementation or system discovery. No evidence found of malware behavior or data collection.

content.user_title: Plan Agile Tasks and Sprint Backlogs
content.value_statement: Teams often turn broad feature ideas into unclear work items. This skill structures features into user stories, acceptance criteria, tasks, priorities, and sprint plans.
actual_capabilities: Creates user story templates with roles, goals, value, acceptance criteria, estimates, dependencies, and priority.
Breaks epics into stories and implementation tasks with time estimates.
Applies MoSCoW prioritization to release and sprint planning decisions.
Builds sprint planning outlines with goals, team capacity, selected stories, and definition of done.
Defines simple task board columns for backlog, active work, review, and completion.
limitations: It does not connect to Jira, Linear, GitHub Issues, or other project tools.
It does not calculate velocity from historical team data.
It provides planning structure, but product owners still validate scope and priority.
Its examples are generic and may need adaptation for regulated or complex domains.

use_cases.title: Break Down a New Feature
use_cases.description: Turn a high-level feature idea into user stories, acceptance criteria, tasks, dependencies, and estimates.

use_cases.title: Prepare Sprint Planning
use_cases.description: Select work for a sprint, align it to capacity, and define a clear sprint goal.

use_cases.title: Clean Up a Backlog
use_cases.description: Rewrite vague backlog items into testable stories and assign priority using MoSCoW categories.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic User Stories
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a single feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the task-planning skill to create user stories for this feature: [feature]. Include acceptance criteria, estimates, dependencies, and priority.

prompt_templates.title: Decompose an Epic
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the task-planning skill to break this epic into stories and tasks: [epic]. Include story points, task estimates, and key dependencies.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Sprint
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the task-planning skill to create a sprint plan for these backlog items: [items]. Team capacity is [capacity]. Include a sprint goal and definition of done.

prompt_templates.title: Prioritize a Release Backlog
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning across multiple delivery phases
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the task-planning skill to prioritize this release backlog with MoSCoW: [backlog]. Explain tradeoffs, sequencing, risks, and what should wait.

output_examples.input: Plan user registration for a web app.
output_examples.output: A user story for new visitor registration, acceptance criteria for valid and invalid signup, technical notes, estimates, dependencies, and must-have priority.

output_examples.input: Break down a user management epic.
output: Stories for registration, login, password reset, profile editing, and email verification.
Tasks for UI, API, validation, tests, and integration work.
Dependencies and estimates for each story.

output_examples.input: Prepare a two-week sprint from a prioritized backlog.
output_examples.output: A sprint goal, selected stories, total estimated points, task board layout, and definition of done checklist.

best_practices: Provide the feature goal, target users, known constraints, and any existing backlog items.
Review generated estimates with the engineering team before committing to sprint scope.
Keep acceptance criteria testable and split stories that exceed normal team size.
anti_patterns: Do not use vague goals such as improve onboarding without measurable outcomes.
Do not accept generated priorities without product and stakeholder review.
Do not combine unrelated user needs into one large story.

faq.question: What planning methods does this skill use?
faq.answer: It uses INVEST user stories, MoSCoW prioritization, sprint planning, and task board structure.

faq.question: Can it create acceptance criteria?
faq.answer: Yes. It creates Given, When, Then criteria that are easier to test and review.

faq.question: Does it integrate with project management tools?
faq.answer: No. It produces planning content that users can move into tools such as Jira, Linear, or GitHub Issues.

faq.question: Can it estimate story points?
faq.answer: Yes. It can suggest points and task estimates, but the team should confirm final sizing.

faq.question: Is it only for Scrum teams?
faq.answer: No. Scrum teams benefit most, but Kanban and general product teams can use the story and backlog structure.

faq.question: What input works best?
faq.answer: Provide the feature description, user type, expected value, constraints, dependencies, and target delivery window.
